segments.text:
Memory Cells
Provide the biological basis for vaccination

Cytokines direct naïve CD4+ cells into either a TH1 or TH2
-Promotes TC growth
-activates macrophages
-Hypersensitivity
-Activates B-cell production of antibodies
-very effectively activates B-cell production of antibodies
At least 90% of the effector cells die by apoptosis after pathogen is cleared
Smallpox  vaccination results in detectable antibody levels for many years
Memory cells associated with CD4 and CD8

There are many different types of T-Memory cells
Stem cell memory (SCM)
Central Memory (CM)
Effector Memory (EM)
Resident Memory (RM)
Model for Development of Memory T-cell subsets

Memory Cells can exist for:
T-cells
B-cells
Natural Killer cells

Secondary response:
Takes far less antigen
Is much quicker and stronger

Important Terms/Concepts
Many different types of Helper T cells
Population of effector cells contracts after elimination of pathogen
Memory cells exist for T-cells, B-cells and Natural Killer Cells
Secondary Response:
Takes less antigen
Stronger and Faster

Naive T-cells differentiate after antigen presentation
Naïve CD4+ or TH cells differentiate into:
TH1-activation of cytotoxic T lymphocytes, 			 	 helper for B-cell activation, hypersensitivity
TH2-very effective helper for B-cell activation
Naïve CD8+, or TC cells differentiate into
Cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs)

Secondary response
Needs far less antigen
Is much faster and stronger

CLP
NK 	        B	      T
TH	                TC
TH1	             TH2
TREG   TH17
